summary: Nadia announces a podcast collaboration with Henry Zhu exploring parallels between faith and open source communities. She observed that Christianity seemed surprisingly prevalent among open source developers, contrasting with the secular tech culture of San Francisco. The podcast examines how both religious communities and open source share values around community, public service, and distributed organization. Through recording ten episodes, Nadia discovered she wasn't as 'non-religious' as she thought, recognizing her own spiritual practice in her drive to amplify creative potential in the world.
key_points: Faith is surprisingly visible in open source communities compared to secular San Francisco tech culture, possibly because both value community and public service
Organized religion functions as 'the original distributed community' with parallels to open source in financing, onboarding newcomers, and building lasting congregations
Discussing religion publicly feels risky because it appends a label to your identity that changes how people see you
The podcast was created as a finite artifact for learning together, challenging the assumption that podcasts must be 'forever' commitments
Through the conversations, Nadia realized her work amplifying creative potential constitutes its own form of spiritual practice
money_quotes: Organized religion is the original distributed community, and it's lasted thousands of years.
Discussing religion can feel like appending a label to your identity, one that will make people see you in an irrevocably different way.

They're organizational systems that bind people tightly together, and whose norms, fascinatingly, didn't derive from any one place, but rather from a shared consensus iterated upon by each passing generation.
We wanted to challenge the idea that podcasts have to be a 'forever' thing.
I have my own version of spiritual practice; I'm just not used to acknowledging it to others.
I'm moved to amplify creative potential in the world, and where I'm in awe of all the strange, ineffable qualities that make us human.
key_insight: Both faith communities and open source are distributed organizational systems built on shared consensus and public service, suggesting deeper structural parallels worth examining.
